---
abstract: |
  This chapter develops neural networks for survival analysis from first principles. We begin with feed-forward neural networks for regression, motivated as a stack of linear models with non-linear activations that can approximate any sufficiently smooth function, and review the key ingredients — layers, activations, mini-batch stochastic gradient descent, backpropagation, and regularization. We then show that adaptation to survival analysis requires no fundamental change to either the architecture or the optimization procedure; it is enough to choose a survival-specific loss function. Three complementary strategies are developed in detail — a parametric strategy that lets the network predict distribution parameters, a semi-parametric Cox-based strategy that uses the partial likelihood as the training objective, and a non-parametric discrete- or piecewise-time strategy that frames hazard estimation as a sequence of interval-wise classification problems. We then turn to two settings where deep learning has a particular comparative advantage — competing risks, where a shared trunk learns one representation from which cause-specific heads branch off, and multi-modal data, where modality-specific encoders feed a common survival head. We close with concise descriptions of the most influential models in the literature — DeepSurv, Cox-Time, DeepHit, Nnet-survival, PC-Hazard and DeepPAMM — each presented as an instantiation of one of the three strategies, and discuss extensions to other censoring and truncation schemes.
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
{{< include _macros.tex >}}
:::

# Neural Networks {#sec-nnet}

{{< include _wip.qmd >}}

*Neural networks* are a flexible class of models that, with enough hidden units, can approximate any sufficiently smooth function [@Hornik1989; @Hornik1991].
General introductions are given in @LeCun2015, @Goodfellow2016 and @Prince2023.
A range of specialized architectures has been developed for different data modalities, most notably *convolutional neural networks* (CNNs) for image data [@LeCun1998; @Krizhevsky2012], *recurrent neural networks* (RNNs) and *long short-term memory* (LSTM) units for sequence data [@Hochreiter1997], and *transformers* based on self-attention for sequence and multi-modal data [@Vaswani2017].

For the purposes of this chapter, the precise architecture is largely orthogonal to the survival adaptation: all of these models share the same mathematical backbone that consists of compositions of linear maps cobmined with non-linear *activations*, trained by minimizing a differentiable loss with stochastic gradient descent.
The adaptation to survival analysis acts on the loss, not on the architecture.
We therefore introduce the basics in terms of the simplest variant, the *feed-forward neural network*, and return to the specialized architectures briefly in @sec-nnet-strengths and @sec-nnet-extensions, where they play a central role in handling images, sequences and multi-modal inputs.

The key insight of this chapter is that the survival-specific likelihoods already encountered throughout this book, such as parametric likelihoods (@sec-surv-estimation-param), the Cox partial likelihood (@sec-classical-cox), the discrete-time likelihood (@sec-discrete-time-reduction), and the piecewise-exponential likelihood (@sec-piecewise-constant-hazards), translate directly into training objectives for neural networks.

The chapter is organized in five parts.
@sec-nnet-regression introduces the essential building blocks of neural networks for continuous regression and establishes the notation used throughout.
@sec-nnet-surv develops three general strategies for using neural networks in survival analysis: parametric, semi-parametric (Cox-based), and non-parametric (discrete- and piecewise-time).
@sec-nnet-strengths then turns to two settings where deep learning has a particular comparative advantage: competing risks with shared representations and multi-modal data fusion.
@sec-nnet-popular gives compact descriptions of the most influential models in the literature, each as an instantiation of one of the three strategies.
@sec-nnet-extensions collects extensions (left-truncation, time-varying effects, alternative architectures) before the chapter concludes with takeaways, limitations, and further reading.

## Neural Networks for Regression {#sec-nnet-regression}

A *feed-forward neural network* (FFNN) maps an input vector $\xx \in \Reals^p$ to an output through a sequence of *layers*.
Each layer applies a linear transformation followed by a non-linear *activation function*.
A network with $L$ hidden layers is defined recursively as
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hh^{(0)} &= \xx \\
\hh^{(l)} &= \sigma^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\hh^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right), \quad l = 1, \ldots, L \\
g(\xx; \bstheta) &= \mathbf{W}^{(L+1)}\hh^{(L)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L+1)},
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-nnet-forward}
where $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ are the weight matrix and bias vector of layer $l$, $\sigma^{(l)}$ is the layer's activation function applied element-wise, and $\bstheta = \{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L+1}$ collects all trainable parameters.
The output $g(\xx; \bstheta) \in \Reals^q$ can be a scalar ($q = 1$) for univariate regression or a vector for multi-output problems.
The graphical structure is illustrated in @fig-ffnn-arch.

![Schematic of a feed-forward neural network with three inputs $x_1, x_2, x_3$, two hidden layers of five and three units $h^{(l)}_j$, and a scalar output $y$. Each circle is a unit; each edge carries a weight in $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$. The output of layer $l$ is the element-wise activation $\sigma$ of the pre-activation $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\hh^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$. Trainable parameters $\bstheta = \{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L+1}$ are learned by minimizing a differentiable loss with stochastic gradient descent.](Figures/neuralnetworks/ffnn-architecture.png){#fig-ffnn-arch fig-alt="A fully connected feed-forward neural network with three input units x_1, x_2, x_3, two hidden layers of five and three units labelled h_j^{(l)}, and a single scalar output y. Edges between adjacent layers indicate the weights." width=85%}

Different activation functions can be used; the most common default for hidden layers is the *rectified linear unit* (ReLU) [@Nair2010], defined by $\sigma(v) = \max(0, v)$.

The *output* layer is treated separately: the activation applied to the last linear transformation is dictated by the natural range of the quantity the network is supposed to predict, not by gradient considerations.
The recursion in @eq-nnet-forward, written as is, applies the identity activation at the output ($g(\xx; \bstheta) \in \Reals^q$ is unbounded), which is appropriate for unconstrained continuous regression.
For other targets a suitable *output activation* is composed with the final linear map:

* a probability in $(0,1)$ uses the sigmoid (binary classification);
* a probability vector over $K$ classes uses the *softmax*, $\operatorname{softmax}(\zz)_k = e^{z_k}/\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}$ (multi-class classification);
* a strictly positive scalar uses the *softplus*, $\sigma(v) = \log(1+e^v)$, or the exponential (e.g., a positive scale parameter such as the Weibull $\lambda$ in @sec-nnet-param);
* a value in a bounded interval uses a scaled / shifted $\tanh$.

The output activation is therefore best thought of as a *link function* in the sense of generalized linear models: it maps the unbounded pre-activation of the last linear layer onto the range required by the prediction target and is implicit in how the loss function consumes the network output (e.g. binary cross-entropy expects a sigmoid output; the discrete-time survival loss of @sec-nnet-discrete expects per-interval sigmoids).

### Training: loss, gradient descent and backpropagation {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Training consists of minimizing a differentiable loss function $\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain)$ with respect to $\bstheta$.
For continuous regression the canonical default is the *mean squared error* (MSE),
$$
\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - g(\xx_i; \bstheta)\bigr)^{2},
$$ {#eq-nnet-mse}
where the network output $\hat{y}_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ is interpreted as a *point prediction* of the response.

#### From point predictions to distributional regression {.unnumbered .unlisted}

For the survival adaptation it is useful to view the MSE not as a free-standing loss but as the negative log-likelihood of a *Normal* observation model with constant variance.
Assuming $Y_i \mid \xx_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ with $\mu_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ and $\sigma^2$ fixed, the per-observation log-density is
$$
\log p(y_i \mid \xx_i) = -\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\bigl(y_i - g(\xx_i; \bstheta)\bigr)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2),
$$
and the maximum-likelihood estimator of $\bstheta$ minimizes precisely @eq-nnet-mse [@Prince2023, ch. 5].
In other words, plain MSE regression is already a probabilistic model — it just happens to fix the variance.

A natural and important generalization is *distributional regression*: instead of predicting only the mean, the network is given a multi-output head that produces *all* parameters of the assumed distribution.
For a Normal model this means the network outputs both the mean and the variance (or a log-variance, to enforce positivity),
$$
\bigl(\hat{\mu}_i,\, \log \hat{\sigma}^2_i\bigr) = g(\xx_i; \bstheta) \in \Reals^2,
$$
and the training objective becomes the negative log-likelihood of the resulting *heteroscedastic* Normal model,
$$
-\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\tfrac{1}{2\hat{\sigma}^2_i}\bigl(y_i - \hat{\mu}_i\bigr)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\log\!\bigl(2\pi\hat{\sigma}^2_i\bigr)\right].
$$ {#eq-nnet-gauss-nll}
The crucial conceptual shift is that the trainable parameters $\bstheta$ now parametrize a whole conditional distribution $p(Y \mid \xx; \bstheta)$ rather than a single conditional mean.
Once a fitted network is available, the prediction for a new observation $\xx^*$ is the full distribution $p(Y \mid \xx^*; \hat{\bstheta})$, from which the mean $\hat{\mu}^* = g(\xx^*; \hat{\bstheta})$ is just one summary among many (one may equally read off quantiles, prediction intervals, or the predictive density at a target value).

Nothing in this generalization is specific to the Normal distribution: any parametric family $p(y \mid \bsphi)$ with a differentiable log-density can be plugged in.
The network outputs the parameter vector $\bsphi_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ (with output activations that respect the parameter constraints — softplus for a positive scale, sigmoid for a probability, etc.), and the loss is the negative log-likelihood $-\sum_i \log p(y_i \mid \bsphi_i)$.
This is the lens we will use to derive the *parametric* neural-network strategy for survival analysis in @sec-nnet-param: replace the Normal with a time-to-event distribution (Weibull, log-normal, …) and replace the i.i.d. log-likelihood with the censored survival log-likelihood; everything else — architecture, optimizer, gradient computation — is unchanged.

#### Classification and the general perspective {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Classification fits into the same picture.
For binary classification with $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ and a sigmoid output, the network parametrizes a *Bernoulli* distribution: write $p_i = \sigma(z_i)$ for the predicted event probability, where $z_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ is the (unconstrained, real-valued) pre-activation of the last linear layer.
The negative log-likelihood is the familiar *binary cross-entropy*
$$
\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl[y_i\log p_i + (1-y_i)\log\bigl(1-p_i\bigr)\bigr].
$$ {#eq-nnet-bce}
For multi-class classification with $K$ classes, a softmax output activation produces a probability vector $\pp_i \in (0,1)^K$ and the *categorical* negative log-likelihood is
$$
\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \II(y_i = k)\,\log p_{ik}.
$$
The general perspective — that the network parametrizes a conditional distribution and is trained by minimizing the negative log-likelihood — is what makes the survival adaptation so direct: switching to a survival problem only requires switching the per-observation contribution, while the architecture, the optimizer and the gradient computation remain unchanged.

#### In practice: logits or probabilities? {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Equation @eq-nnet-bce treats the probability $p_i = \sigma(z_i)$ as the network output.
In an actual implementation, however, the network is almost always defined to return the *unbounded* pre-activation $z_i$ — the *logit* — and the sigmoid is folded into the loss.
Two reasons drive this convention.
First, evaluating $\log \sigma(z)$ and $\log(1-\sigma(z))$ directly suffers from numerical overflow and underflow for $|z|$ large, whereas the algebraically equivalent forms
$$
\log \sigma(z) = -\log\!\bigl(1+e^{-z}\bigr), \qquad \log\bigl(1-\sigma(z)\bigr) = -\log\!\bigl(1+e^{z}\bigr),
$$
are stable through the standard log-sum-exp trick.
Second, splitting the sigmoid off the network keeps the building blocks composable: the same logit can be combined with different loss functions, and a single fused "loss-from-logits" operator can be optimized inside the framework.
Concretely, in PyTorch one omits `nn.Sigmoid` from the network's final layer and uses `nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss` instead of applying `nn.BCELoss` to an explicit sigmoid output; the multi-class analogue is `nn.CrossEntropyLoss`, which consumes the raw $K$-dimensional logits directly.
The same pattern reappears across this chapter's survival losses: networks for Cox-based models (@sec-nnet-cox) emit an unbounded log-risk score, networks for discrete-time models (@sec-nnet-discrete) emit per-interval logits that are passed through sigmoids inside the loss, and parametric networks (@sec-nnet-param) emit log-parameters such as $\log\lambda$ and $\log k$ that are exponentiated inside the Weibull log-likelihood — see the example in @fig-weibull-aft-nn for a concrete instance.

Optimization typically uses *stochastic gradient descent* (SGD) or adaptive variants such as Adam [@Kingma2015].
In each iteration, parameters are updated by a small step in the direction of the negative gradient,
$$
\bstheta^{t+1} = \bstheta^{t} - \alpha\, \nabla_{\bstheta}\, \widetilde{\ell}\bigl(\bstheta^{t}\bigr),
$$
where the *learning rate* $\alpha > 0$ controls the step size, and $\widetilde{\ell}$ is a noisy estimate of the full loss computed on a *mini-batch* — a random subset $B \subset \{1,\ldots,n\}$,
$$
\widetilde{\ell}(\bstheta) = \frac{1}{|B|}\sum_{i \in B} \ell_i(\bstheta).
$$
Mini-batching reduces the per-step memory and compute cost from $O(n)$ to $O(|B|)$ and injects beneficial stochasticity into the gradient estimates [@Goodfellow2016].
The gradient $\nabla_{\bstheta}\,\ell$ itself is computed efficiently by *backpropagation*, which applies the chain rule layer by layer from the output back to the input.
Backpropagation is the *algorithm* used to compute gradients; SGD (or Adam) is the *update rule* that uses those gradients to take a step.

### Regularization and hyper-parameters {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Neural networks are over-parameterized by design and would overfit small datasets without regularization.
Two regularization strategies are widely used.
*Weight decay* adds a penalty $\lambda\|\bstheta\|_2^2$ to the loss for some $\lambda > 0$, identical to the ridge penalty in the linear case.
*Dropout* [@Srivastava2014] randomly sets a fraction $p_{\text{drop}}$ of activations to zero during each training step, which can be interpreted as implicit ensembling over thinned sub-networks.
*Early stopping* — monitoring a held-out validation loss and terminating training once it stops improving — provides an additional, essentially free, form of regularization.

The principal hyper-parameters of an FFNN are therefore the depth $L$, the width of each layer, the learning rate $\alpha$, the dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$, the weight-decay coefficient $\lambda$, and the mini-batch size $|B|$.
They are usually selected by cross-validation or by monitoring a held-out validation set; an exhaustive treatment is given in @Goodfellow2016 and @Bishop2006.

### Beyond fully-connected networks {.unnumbered .unlisted}

The recursion in @eq-nnet-forward defines fully-connected feed-forward networks, but the same training machinery — differentiable loss, backpropagation, mini-batch SGD — applies to any differentiable architecture.
*Convolutional* networks (CNNs) exploit local spatial structure and are the default choice for images.
*Recurrent* networks (RNNs) and *transformers* process sequences of arbitrary length and are used for longitudinal covariates and text.
*Autoencoders* and their variational extensions learn compact representations of high-dimensional inputs.
For survival analysis these architectures are largely treated as drop-in replacements for the feature extractor $g(\,\cdot\,; \bstheta)$: the survival-specific loss function is applied on top of whichever architecture is most natural for the input data.

## Neural Networks for Survival Analysis {#sec-nnet-surv}

Neural networks are not inherently designed for survival data; they expect a fixed-dimensional output and a differentiable scalar loss.
The adaptation to survival analysis therefore rests on two choices: what the network outputs and which loss function is minimized.
The three strategies introduced below correspond directly to the three main inferential frameworks established earlier in this book.

A critical point is that naïve adaptations — treating the event indicator $\delta_i$ as a binary classification target or the observed time $t_i$ as a regression target while ignoring censoring — are generally invalid, because they discard the information carried by censored observations (see @sec-classical-cox for a detailed discussion).
All three strategies below correctly handle right-censored data; extensions to other censoring and truncation schemes are discussed in @sec-nnet-extensions.

### Parametric Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-param}

The most direct extension of standard regression neural networks to survival analysis is to assume a parametric distribution for the event time $T$ and to maximize the corresponding log-likelihood.
This is exactly the distributional-regression construction of @eq-nnet-gauss-nll, with the Normal density replaced by a time-to-event density and with the log-likelihood adjusted for censoring.

Recall from @sec-surv-estimation-param that, for right-censored data, the log-likelihood of a parametric model with subject-specific distribution parameters $\bsphi_i$ is
$$
\ell(\bsphi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \log f(t_i \mid \bsphi_i) + (1 - \delta_i) \log S(t_i \mid \bsphi_i)\right].
$$ {#eq-nnet-param-lik}

In a neural network formulation, the network $g(\,\cdot\,; \bstheta): \Reals^p \to \Reals^q$ predicts the distribution parameters $\bsphi_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ directly from the covariates.
For a Weibull distribution, for example, the network outputs two positive scalars, the scale $\lambda_i > 0$ and the shape $k_i > 0$, with positivity ensured by a softplus or exponential output activation.
The training objective is then
$$
-\ell(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \log f_{\Weib}(t_i \mid \lambda_i, k_i) + (1 - \delta_i) \log S_{\Weib}(t_i \mid \lambda_i, k_i)\right],
$$
and standard backpropagation applies because $f_{\Weib}$ and $S_{\Weib}$ are differentiable in their parameters.

This approach is very general: any distribution with a closed-form density and survival function can be used, including the exponential, log-normal, log-logistic, or Gompertz distributions, as well as mixtures and spline-based distributions that allow flexible hazard shapes.
Once the network is trained, a full predicted survival distribution $\hatS(\cdot \mid \xx^*)$ is available for each new observation $\xx^*$, enabling calibrated probabilistic predictions.

The parametric approach requires committing to a distributional assumption.
Misspecification of the distribution can bias predictions, particularly in the tails of the survival function.
However, the gain is a coherent probabilistic output and typically better statistical efficiency than non-parametric alternatives when the assumed distribution is approximately correct.

#### Example: Weibull AFT on the tumor data {.unnumbered .unlisted}

To make the construction concrete, we fit a Weibull AFT in three increasingly flexible parametrisations to the `tumor` data set (@tbl-surv-data-tumor) and contrast the predicted survival curves with the corresponding Kaplan-Meier estimate stratified by `complications`.
All three variants use the same Weibull likelihood and the same Adam optimizer; they differ only in *which* parameters depend on covariates and *how flexibly* the dependence is parametrised:

* **M1** — only the scale depends on the single covariate `complications`; the shape $k$ is a single global parameter shared across all subjects. The network's "scale head" is a linear map from a one-dimensional input, the "shape head" is a global scalar.
* **M2** — both scale and shape depend on `complications`, each through a separate linear head.
* **M3** — both scale and shape depend on *all seven* features in the dataset, each through a one-hidden-layer MLP (16 ReLU units), yielding fully *individualised* predicted distributions.

To keep the optimisation well-behaved on a sample of 776 patients we constrain each head's output through a `tanh` activation, anchoring the scale around the marginal Weibull MLE and bounding the shape away from extreme values; without such a bound the more flexible MLP heads can produce pathological per-subject parameter combinations (a small number of subjects with $\hat\lambda_i \to \infty$ or $\hat k_i \gg 1$) that obscure the otherwise smooth structure of the individualised curves.

@fig-weibull-aft-nn shows the predicted survival curves for the three variants.

![Weibull AFT fit to the `tumor` data via a neural network, in three increasingly flexible parametrisations. *Left (M1)*: only $\log\lambda$ depends on `complications`; the shape $k$ is global, so the two predicted curves share the same shape and differ only in scale. *Middle (M2)*: both $\log\lambda$ and $\log k$ depend on `complications`; the predicted curves can now bend differently, and the no-complications curve flattens earlier than in M1. *Right (M3)*: both heads are MLPs over all seven covariates and produce one Weibull distribution *per patient*; the resulting fan of individualised predicted curves is coloured by complications status, with the Kaplan-Meier estimates overlaid for reference.](Figures/neuralnetworks/weibull-aft-nn.png){#fig-weibull-aft-nn fig-alt="Three side-by-side panels showing predicted survival curves vs. Kaplan-Meier estimates stratified by complications. Left and middle panels each show two AFT curves (no complications in blue, complications in red) against dotted KM step curves; the middle panel's curves bend more flexibly. The right panel shows 776 individualised AFT curves coloured by complications group, fanned out around the KM curves." width=100%}

The figure illustrates two pedagogical points.
First, *which* parameters depend on the features matters: M1 imposes proportional decay (same shape, scaled time), M2 lifts that restriction by letting the shape vary across groups, and M3 lets each individual carry their own $(\lambda_i, k_i)$ pair.
Second, despite the very different output structures of the three variants, the underlying model class — Weibull AFT — and the training procedure are identical; only the architecture of the parameter-emitting heads is changed.
The full PyTorch source for the figure is in `book/experiments/code.py`.

### Semi-Parametric: Cox-based Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-cox}

The Cox proportional hazards model (@sec-classical-cox) avoids the distributional assumption by specifying only the relative hazard through a linear predictor $\eta_i = \xx_i^\trans \bsbeta$.
This semi-parametric structure carries over naturally to neural networks: instead of a linear predictor, a neural network $g(\,\cdot\,; \bstheta): \Reals^p \to \Reals$ outputs a scalar risk score $\eta_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ and the Cox partial likelihood is used as the training objective.

The negative partial log-likelihood (cf. @eq-lpartial) is
$$
-\ell^{PH}(\bstheta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i\left[g(\xx_i; \bstheta) - \log\!\left(\sum_{j \in \calR_{t_i}} \exp(g(\xx_j; \bstheta))\right)\right]
$$ {#eq-nnet-cox-loss}
where $\calR_{t_i} = \{j : t_j \geq t_i\}$ is the risk set at time $t_i$.
This loss is differentiable in $\bstheta$ and can be minimized by backpropagation.

The Cox-based approach predicts a risk score $\hatg(\xx^*) = f_{\hat{\bstheta}}(\xx^*)$, a ranking quantity analogous to $\hat{\eta}$ in the Cox model.
To obtain a full predicted survival distribution, the Breslow estimator of the baseline cumulative hazard $\hatH_0$ is combined with the predicted risk score, exactly as in the standard Cox model (@sec-classical-cox).

A key advantage of the Cox-based approach is the partial likelihood's robustness: it requires no assumption about the shape of the baseline hazard.
The computational cost of @eq-nnet-cox-loss, however, scales quadratically with $n$ in the worst case because of the double sum over risk sets.
This is most often addressed by *risk-set sub-sampling*: at each iteration the risk set $\calR_{t_i}$ is replaced by its intersection with the current mini-batch, which yields a stochastic but unbiased gradient estimate for the partial likelihood gradient on average [@Katzman2018; @Kvamme2019a].

### Non-Parametric: Discrete- and Piecewise-Time Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-discrete}

The third strategy makes no parametric or proportional-hazards assumption and instead discretizes the follow-up period, framing survival estimation as a sequence of interval-wise classification or Poisson-regression problems, as introduced in @sec-partition-based-reductions.

Given a partition $\mathcal{A} = \{a_0, \ldots, a_J\}$ of the follow-up period into $J$ intervals $(a_{j-1}, a_j]$, let $J_i$ be the index of the interval in which observation $i$'s outcome falls.
The *discrete hazard* in interval $j$ conditional on covariates $\xx$ is
$$
h^d(j \mid \xx) = P(T \in (a_{j-1}, a_j] \mid T > a_{j-1}, \xx),
$$
and the corresponding discrete survival function is
$$
S^d(j \mid \xx) = \prod_{k=1}^{j}\bigl(1 - h^d(k \mid \xx)\bigr).
$$

A neural network with $J$ output units and sigmoid activations can predict these $J$ discrete hazards simultaneously:
$$
\left(\hat{h}^d(1 \mid \xx_i), \ldots, \hat{h}^d(J \mid \xx_i)\right) = \sigma\!\left(g(\xx_i; \bstheta)\right) \in (0,1)^J.
$$
The discrete-time log-likelihood (@eq-discrete-likelihood-1) then serves as the training objective:
$$
-\ell^{d}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\sum_{j=1}^{J_i - 1}\log\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx_i)\bigr) + \delta_i \log \hat{h}^d(J_i \mid \xx_i) + (1-\delta_i)\log\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(J_i \mid \xx_i)\bigr)\right].
$$ {#eq-nnet-discrete-loss}

This is precisely the binary cross-entropy loss applied interval-by-interval, which is a standard and well-supported loss in deep learning frameworks.
Once trained, the full predicted survival curve is recovered as the running product $\hatS(\tau \mid \xx^*) = \prod_{j : a_j \leq \tau}\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx^*)\bigr)$.

The number of intervals $J$ and the placement of interval boundaries $\mathcal{A}$ are important modeling choices, as discussed in @sec-choice-of-interval-boundaries.
A fine partition allows more flexible hazard estimation but increases the output dimension and may require more training data; a coarse partition is more parsimonious but may miss important temporal patterns.

A close continuous-time alternative uses the *piecewise exponential model* (PEM, @sec-piecewise-constant-hazards), in which the hazard is assumed constant within each interval but the exact event and at-risk times within the interval are preserved.
The Poisson-form likelihood of the PEM is the training objective and the network output is the log-hazard in each interval rather than a discrete hazard.
This avoids the information loss incurred by replacing the exact event time $t_i$ with the interval index $J_i$ and provides continuous-time predictions $\hatS(\tau \mid \xx^*)$ for any $\tau$ via the piecewise cumulative hazard.

Both variants make no distributional assumptions, can model arbitrary time-varying covariate effects by including the interval index $j$ (or the interval midpoint $a_{j-1/2}$) as an input feature, and are implemented using losses that are standard in all major deep learning frameworks.
They are also the direct deep learning instantiation of the partition-based reduction techniques (@sec-partition-based-reductions): the neural network serves as the (multi-output) classification or Poisson-regression model whose targets are the interval-wise event indicators of the transformed data.

## Strengths of Deep Learning for Survival Analysis {#sec-nnet-strengths}

The three strategies above already make neural networks competitive with classical and tree-based models on tabular survival data, but they do not yet exhibit a clear comparative advantage [@Wiegrebe2024].
Two settings, however, play to neural networks' specific strengths: settings with multiple competing event types, where a *shared representation* can be learned once and re-used by cause-specific heads, and *multi-modal* data, where modality-specific encoders (CNNs for images, MLPs for tabular data, etc.) can be plugged in and trained jointly under a single survival objective.

### Competing Risks via Shared Networks {#sec-nnet-cr}

In the competing-risks setting (@sec-competing-risks) a subject can transition to one of $Q$ mutually exclusive terminal states, with cause-specific hazards $h_q(t \mid \xx)$ for $q = 1,\ldots,Q$.
A naïve neural-network solution is to train $K$ separate single-event networks, one per cause, each ignoring observations whose event is of a different cause (treating them as censored at the time of the competing event).
The richer alternative — and the dominant pattern in the deep-learning–for-survival literature [@Wiegrebe2024] — is to share parameters across causes by stacking a common *trunk* that maps inputs to a shared representation, followed by $Q$ cause-specific *heads* that produce the cause-specific predictions (@fig-shared-trunk-cr).

![Shared-trunk architecture for competing risks. A common feature extractor maps the covariates $\xx$ to a learned representation, which is then routed into $Q$ cause-specific heads producing the cause-specific hazards $\hat{h}_q(t \mid \xx)$ or, equivalently, the cause-specific cumulative incidence functions. The shared trunk encourages parameter sharing across causes — a form of multi-task learning that typically improves sample efficiency when the same covariates are predictive of multiple event types.](Figures/neuralnetworks/shared-trunk-cr.png){#fig-shared-trunk-cr fig-alt="A diagram showing input features routed into a single shared trunk of hidden layers, which then branches into Q parallel cause-specific heads, each producing a cause-specific hazard estimate." width=90%}

Formally, decompose the network as $g(\xx; \bstheta) = (\psi_1(\phi(\xx)), \ldots, \psi_Q(\phi(\xx)))$, where $\phi$ is the shared trunk and $\psi_q$ are the cause-specific heads.
Training is by joint minimization of the sum of cause-specific losses, e.g.
$$
-\ell_{\text{CR}}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \left[-\ell^{(q)}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain)\right],
$$
where $\ell^{(q)}$ is the cause-$q$ contribution under one of the three strategies of @sec-nnet-surv (the cause-specific partial likelihood for a Cox-based head, a discrete-time log-likelihood with softmax-over-causes for a discrete-time head, or a parametric sub-distribution log-likelihood).
For the discrete-time strategy, the head is most naturally parametrized as a $J \times (Q+1)$ output matrix with a softmax over causes (the extra column being the "no event in $j$" probability), as in *DeepHit* [@Lee2018a]; for the Cox-based strategy, one head per cause is trained on the cause-specific partial likelihood; and for the piecewise-exponential strategy, the heads predict cause-specific log-hazards on the shared partition $\mathcal{A}$.

The shared-trunk pattern is an instance of *multi-task learning*: the trunk is encouraged to learn features that are useful for predicting all causes simultaneously, which acts as a form of regularization and typically improves sample efficiency when the same covariates are predictive of multiple event types.
Beyond competing risks, the same pattern extends to *multi-state* models (@sec-multi-state), where one head is added per transition; *recurrent events*, where additional outputs predict the time and type of the next event; and to joint modelling of event time with auxiliary outcomes (e.g. tumour response).

### Multi-modal Data {#sec-nnet-multimodal}

A second comparative advantage of neural networks is their ability to learn jointly from heterogeneous *modalities*: tabular clinical features, medical images, free text, gene-expression vectors, or wearable-device time series.
Each modality has its own natural architectural prior — convolutional networks for images, transformers for text, MLPs for tabular features — but all of them produce real-valued embeddings that can be concatenated (or otherwise fused) and fed into a single survival head.
The whole network is trained end-to-end against a single survival loss, so the modality-specific encoders learn features that are directly informative for the survival outcome rather than for a generic upstream task (@fig-multimodal-fusion).

![Multi-modal survival architecture. Each modality is processed by a dedicated encoder — for example, a multi-layer perceptron for tabular features, a convolutional network for images, and an autoencoder or MLP for omics vectors — whose outputs are concatenated (or fused with an attention mechanism) and passed to a shared survival head. The full network is trained end-to-end against a single survival loss, so each encoder learns representations that are directly informative for the survival outcome.](Figures/neuralnetworks/multimodal-fusion.png){#fig-multimodal-fusion fig-alt="A diagram showing three input modalities — tabular data, images, and omics vectors — fed into three modality-specific encoders. The encoder outputs are concatenated in a fusion block, which feeds into a single shared survival head." width=95%}

Formally, generalize the linear predictor from a single covariate vector $\xx$ to a tuple $(\xx, \mathbf{z}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{z}_G)$ of modalities and write
$$
\eta_i = f(\xx_i) + \sum_{g=1}^{G} d_g(\mathbf{z}_{ig}),
$$ {#eq-multimodal}
where $f$ is the tabular encoder and $d_g$ are the modality-specific embedding networks for $G$ unstructured modalities (image, text, omics, …) [@Wiegrebe2024].
The right-hand side of @eq-multimodal is then fed into any of the three survival loss functions of @sec-nnet-surv.

This composability is the main practical advantage of neural networks over other survival models: while classical and tree-based models can in principle be applied to flattened images or one-hot text, they cannot exploit the local or sequential structure of the raw signal.
Concrete instantiations in the literature include CNNs on histopathology slides combined with structured demographic and molecular features [@Wiegrebe2024], pre-trained ResNets on radiological images with tabular clinical features, and joint modelling of multi-omics inputs through modality-specific autoencoders followed by a Cox loss.

## Popular Models {#sec-nnet-popular}

Each of the three strategies developed in @sec-nnet-surv has well-known instantiations in the literature.
The descriptions below cover the most influential models and locate each within the taxonomy already established.

### DeepSurv and Faraggi–Simon (Cox-based) {.unnumbered}

The earliest neural-Cox model was the single-hidden-layer network of @Faraggi1995, in which the linear predictor of the Cox model was replaced by a small neural network and the partial likelihood was used as the training objective.
*DeepSurv* [@Katzman2018] extended this to deep multi-layer perceptrons, added dropout and weight decay for regularization, and demonstrated competitive performance on a number of benchmark datasets.
Implementations exist in the `pycox` Python package [@pkgpycox] and in major deep-learning frameworks [@pkgkeras; @pkgtensorflow].
DeepSurv is, in essence, the natural deep-learning analogue of the Cox model (@sec-classical-cox) and is the canonical example of the Cox-based strategy of @sec-nnet-cox.

### Cox-Time (Cox-based, non-proportional) {.unnumbered}

*Cox-Time* [@Kvamme2019a] relaxes the proportional-hazards assumption by allowing the network to take time as an additional input, $g(\xx_i, t; \bstheta)$, so that the relative risk score can vary with time.
The resulting hazard $h(t \mid \xx) = h_0(t)\exp\bigl(g(\xx, t; \bstheta)\bigr)$ accommodates non-proportional hazard patterns; a modified loss replaces the exact risk set with a subset sampled from the mini-batch, which keeps training mini-batchable.
Cox-Time is the most popular Cox-based deep model to support time-varying effects.

### DeepHit (discrete-time, competing risks) {.unnumbered}

*DeepHit* [@Lee2018a] is the most widely used discrete-time survival network.
It parametrizes the joint distribution over event times and causes as a multinomial output over $J \cdot Q + 1$ categories (one for each $(j, q)$ pair plus a "no event by end of follow-up" category) using a softmax activation.
The loss augments the discrete-time log-likelihood of @eq-nnet-discrete-loss with a ranking term that directly optimizes concordance,
$$
L_{\text{rank}}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{i \neq j} \delta_i \,\II(t_i < t_j)\,\sigma\!\left(\frac{\hatS(t_i \mid \xx_i) - \hatS(t_i \mid \xx_j)}{\omega}\right)
$$
for a bandwidth parameter $\omega > 0$.
The total loss is a convex combination $\alpha\,(-\ell^d) + (1-\alpha)\,L_{\text{rank}}$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is a hyper-parameter.
DeepHit was originally designed for competing risks via the shared-trunk pattern of @sec-nnet-cr and remains a standard baseline whenever competing risks are present.

### Nnet-survival (discrete-time) {.unnumbered}

*Nnet-survival* [@Gensheimer2019] is a minimalist discrete-time network: the output layer has $J$ sigmoidal units producing the discrete hazards $\hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx)$, and the loss is the plain discrete-time log-likelihood of @eq-nnet-discrete-loss.
Without the ranking term of DeepHit and with no restriction on the network architecture, Nnet-survival is the most direct deep-learning analogue of the discrete-time reduction technique (@sec-discrete-time-reduction).
By including the interval index as a feature it can model time-varying covariate effects, and by stacking convolutional or recurrent layers it can also handle images or sequences.

### PC-Hazard (piecewise exponential) {.unnumbered}

*PC-Hazard* [@Kvamme2021] is a piecewise-exponential (PEM) deep model: a single network outputs the log-hazard in each interval of a fixed partition, and training minimizes the Poisson-form PEM negative log-likelihood (cf. @sec-piecewise-constant-hazards).
Unlike DeepHit and Nnet-survival, PC-Hazard preserves the exact event and at-risk times within each interval and therefore avoids the information loss of pure discrete-time methods.

### DeepPAMM (piecewise additive mixed model) {.unnumbered}

*DeepPAMM* [@Kopper2021] is a further generalization of the PEM strategy that combines the structured additive predictor of piecewise-exponential additive mixed models with deep neural-network components.
It supports time-varying effects (by interacting the network output with the interval index), left-truncation (by adjusting the risk-time accumulation in the PEM likelihood), competing risks and multi-state outcomes (via the multi-state interpretation of the PEM), recurrent events (via random effects), and multi-modal inputs (via the additive structured predictor in @eq-multimodal).
It is the most "task-complete" of the deep-survival models in the sense of @Wiegrebe2024 and serves as a useful unifying reference point connecting the deep-learning strategies of this chapter to the partition-based reduction techniques of @sec-partition-based-reductions.

## Extensions {#sec-nnet-extensions}

### Left-truncation {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Left-truncation (@sec-left-truncation) is accommodated by adjusting each likelihood contribution.
For the parametric approach, the log-likelihood @eq-nnet-param-lik becomes
$$
\ell(\bstheta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\delta_i \log f(t_i \mid \bsphi_i) + (1 - \delta_i) \log S(t_i \mid \bsphi_i) - \log S(l_i \mid \bsphi_i)\right],
$$
where $l_i$ is the left-truncation time of observation $i$, conditioning on survival to entry into the study.
The same adjustment applies to the discrete-time likelihood.
For the Cox partial likelihood, the risk sets $\calR_{t_i}$ are updated to include only observations that were at risk at time $t_i$ given their truncation times, $\calR_{t_i} = \{j : l_j \leq t_i \leq t_j\}$, as in @sec-surv-estimation-param.

### Time-varying covariates and effects {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Time-varying covariates are accommodated by reshaping the data so that each observation is replaced by a sequence of $(t_{\text{start}}, t_{\text{end}}, \xx(t))$ tuples and by feeding the time-stamped covariates into a recurrent or transformer-based encoder.
Time-varying *effects* — coefficients that depend on $t$ — are accommodated either explicitly, by feeding the time argument as an extra input (as in Cox-Time and the time-as-feature version of Nnet-survival), or implicitly, by using a discrete- or piecewise-time network with sufficiently expressive interval-specific outputs.

### Other architectures {.unnumbered .unlisted}

All three strategies apply to any differentiable network architecture.
Convolutional architectures are the default when the input is an image, e.g. in medical imaging applications; recurrent and transformer-based architectures are appropriate for longitudinal or sequential inputs; autoencoders and their variational extensions provide compact representations of high-dimensional inputs such as gene-expression profiles.
In all cases, the survival-specific loss function is unchanged; only the network's feature extractor changes.
This architectural flexibility is, together with the multi-modal composability of @sec-nnet-multimodal, the main practical advantage of neural networks over other survival models [@Wiegrebe2024].

## Conclusion

:::: {.callout-warning icon=false}

## Key takeaways

* Neural networks are adapted to survival analysis by pairing a suitable network architecture with a survival-aware loss function derived from the same likelihoods used in classical and semi-parametric models.
* Three complementary strategies exist: parametric (distributional output), semi-parametric Cox-based (ranking output), and non-parametric discrete- or piecewise-time (direct hazard estimation). The choice depends on modelling assumptions and the type of prediction required.
* All three strategies are compatible with standard deep-learning frameworks and differ from the vanilla regression/classification case only in their loss function.
* Competing risks and multi-modal data are the two settings in which neural networks have a clear comparative advantage: shared-trunk architectures enable parameter sharing across causes, and modality-specific encoders compose freely under a single survival objective.
* The discrete- and piecewise-time strategies are the direct deep-learning analogues of the partition-based reduction techniques (@sec-partition-based-reductions); the neural network can be used as the (multi-output) classification or Poisson-regression model in those reductions.

::::

:::: {.callout-important icon=false}

## Limitations

* Neural networks are data-hungry: unlike classical survival models they tend to require larger sample sizes to achieve good performance, particularly when the event rate is low.
* Training stability is sensitive to hyper-parameter choices (learning rate, architecture, regularization). Careful tuning and multiple random restarts are often necessary.
* Predictions from neural networks are difficult to interpret compared to, e.g., the Cox model, where coefficients have a direct hazard-ratio interpretation.
* The computational cost of the Cox partial likelihood (@eq-nnet-cox-loss) over large risk sets can be prohibitive; mini-batch risk-set sub-sampling introduces noise that may affect convergence.
* Many deep survival methods address only single-risk, right-censored data on a single modality; left-truncation, interval-censoring and recurrent events remain comparatively underdeveloped [@Wiegrebe2024].

::::

:::: {.callout-tip icon=false}

## Further reading

* @Goodfellow2016 and @Bishop2006 for general-purpose treatments of neural networks and deep learning.
* @Wiegrebe2024 for a comprehensive review of deep learning for survival analysis covering architectures, loss functions and benchmark comparisons; the companion table at <https://survival-org.github.io/DL4Survival> indexes over 60 methods along the taxonomy used in this chapter.
* @chen2024Introduction for a self-contained introduction to deep survival analysis, covering classical and modern approaches including competing risks and dynamic settings.
* @Faraggi1995 and @Katzman2018 for the neural-Cox approach (DeepSurv); @Kvamme2019a for Cox-Time and a unified `pycox` implementation [@pkgpycox].
* @Lee2018a for DeepHit and the competing-risks discrete-time approach.
* @Gensheimer2019 for Nnet-survival; @Biganzoli1998 for the earlier PLANN model that pioneered discrete-time neural survival.
* @Kvamme2021 for PC-Hazard; @Kopper2021 for DeepPAMM and the unification with piecewise-exponential additive models.
* @pkgkeras and @pkgtensorflow for general deep-learning frameworks; @pkgsksurvival for a classical-style survival library that interoperates with neural-network–based models.

::::
